title: Fiscal policy
description: In this exercise, practice what you've learned about how taxes and government spending can be used as fiscal policy tools to close output gaps.

assessment_items.question_content:
The marginal propensity to consume in the nation of Erd is $0.75$

**What is the value of the government spending multiplier and what is the value of the tax multiplier?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which of the following would be a government spending and tax multiplier for a country?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Government spending multiplier equal 9 and tax multiplier equals -8
Government spending multiplier equal 10 and tax multiplier equals -5
Government spending multiplier equal 12 and tax multiplier equals -15
Government spending multiplier equal 3 and tax multiplier equals -4
Government spending multiplier equal -9 and tax multiplier equals -9

assessment_items.question_content:
Xela's economy was in long-run equilibrium but experienced a shock as shown in this graph:

[[☃ image 1]]

**If the government of Xela wants to use a discretionary fiscal policy tool to return the economy to full employment output, which of the following would be the most appropriate choice?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

One downward-sloping line from the top left to the bottom right is labeled A D. assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is a tool of discretionary fiscal policy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Government spending on a new infrastructure project
Automatic stabilizers such as income taxes

assessment_items.question_content:
The economy of Maxistan was in long-run equilibrium, but it experienced a shock shown in this graph:

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following would be the most appropriate discretionary fiscal policy action to restore Maxistan to full employment output?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A coordinate plane plots five labeled straight lines: A D subscript 1, A D subscript 2, S R A S, L R A S, and two horizontal dashed lines at P L subscript 1 and P L subscript 2. The horizontal axis is labeled r G D P, and the vertical axis is labeled P L. The axes are unmarked. Lines A D subscript 1 and A D subscript 2 are downward-sloping from top left to bottom right, with A D subscript 2 to the right of A D subscript 1. Line S R A S is upward-sloping from bottom left to top right. Line L R A S is vertical and intersects both aggregate demand curves. A rightward arrow points from A D subscript 1 to A D subscript 2. Horizontal dashed lines extend leftward from P L subscript 1 and P L subscript 2. Vertical dashed lines drop from the intersection points to the horizontal axis at Y subscript 1 and Y subscript 2.
options.caption: The economy of Maxistan before and after a shock

assessment_items.question_content:
Before experiencing the supply shock shown in this graph, Maxistan was in long-run equilibrium. The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) Maxistan is $0.75$.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following is a fiscal policy action that would be the best choice to return the economy to full employment output?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Maxistan has a negative output gap of $\$75$ million. In order to close this gap, real GDP must increase by $\$20$ million.

A marginal propensity to consume of $0.75$ means that the government spending multiplier will be $4$:

$\begin{align} \text{government spending multiplier}&=\dfrac{1}{1-MPC}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{1-0.75}
\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{0.25}\\
\\
&=4
\end{align}$

The tax multiplier is always one less than the government spending multiplier, and negative:

$\begin{align} \text{tax multiplier}&=-\dfrac{MPC}{MPS}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{0.75}{0.25}\\\\
&=-3\end{align}$

To find out how much of a change is necessary, divide the amount of the change needed to close a gap by the appropriate multiplier:

$\begin{align}\text{Amount of government spending needed}&=\dfrac{\text{size of gap}}{\text{government spending multiplier}}\\
\\
\\
\text{Amount of tax change needed}&=\dfrac{\text{gap to close}}{\text{tax multiplier}}
\end{align}$

options.alt: A coordinate plane plots five labeled straight lines: A D, S R A S subscript 1, S R A S subscript 2, and L R A S, with two horizontal dashed lines at P L subscript 1 and P L subscript 2. The horizontal axis is labeled r G D P in million dollar sign, and the vertical axis is labeled P L. The axes are marked with dollar sign 125 and dollar sign 200 on the horizontal axis. Line A D is downward-sloping from top left to bottom right. Lines S R A S subscript 1 and S R A S subscript 2 are upward-sloping from bottom left to top right, with S R A S subscript 2 to the left of S R A S subscript 1. Line L R A S is vertical and intersects A D with S R A S subscript 1 at dollar sign 200 and with S R A S subscript 2 at dollar sign 125. Dashed lines extend horizontally from P L subscript 1 and P L subscript 2 and vertically from the two output levels to their respective intersections.

assessment_items.question_content:
The economy shown here has experienced a negative aggregate demand shock and is currently in a recession. The marginal propensity to consume is $0.8$

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following is a fiscal policy action that would be the best choice to return the economy to full employment output?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: This economy has a negative output gap of $\$100$ million. In order to close this gap, real GDP must increase by $\$100$ million.

A marginal propensity to consume of $0.8$ means that the government spending multiplier will be $5$:

$\begin{align} \text{government spending multiplier}&=\dfrac{1}{1-MPC}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{1-0.8}
\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{0.2}\\
\\
&=5
\end{align}$

The tax multiplier is always one less than the government spending multiplier, and negative:

$\begin{align} \text{tax multiplier}&=-\dfrac{MPC}{MPS}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{0.8}{0.2}\\\\
&=-4\end{align}$

To find out how much of a change is necessary, divide the amount of the change needed to close a gap by the appropriate multiplier:

$\begin{align}\text{Amount of government spending needed}&=\dfrac{\text{size of gap}}{\text{government spending multiplier}}\\
\\
\\
\text{Amount of tax change needed}&=\dfrac{\text{gap to close}}{\text{tax multiplier}}
\end{align}$

options.alt: A coordinate plane plots five labeled straight lines: A D subscript 1, A D subscript 2, S R A S, L R A S, and two horizontal dashed lines at price levels 105 and 110. The horizontal axis is labeled r G D P, and the vertical axis is labeled P L. The axes are marked with dollar sign 250 m and dollar sign 350 m. Lines A D subscript 1 and A D subscript 2 are downward-sloping from top left to bottom right, with A D subscript 2 to the left of A D subscript 1. Line S R A S is upward-sloping from bottom left to top right. Line L R A S is vertical and intersects both A D subscript 2 and S R A S at dollar sign 350 m. A leftward arrow points from A D subscript 1 to A D subscript 2. Horizontal dashed lines extend from price levels 105 and 110. Vertical dashed lines drop from dollar sign 250 m and dollar sign 350 m to the horizontal axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose that an economy is in the middle of a recession and government policy makers want to increase aggregate demand by $\$800$ billion. 

**If the economy’s marginal propensity to consume is $0.9$ and there is no crowding out, the government should do which of the following fiscal policy actions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A marginal propensity to consume of $0.9$ means that the government spending multiplier will be $10$:

$\begin{align} \text{government spending multiplier}&=\dfrac{1}{1-MPC}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{1-0.9}
\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{0.1}\\
\\
&=10
\end{align}$

The tax multiplier is always one less than the government spending multiplier, and negative:

$\begin{align} \text{tax multiplier}&=-\dfrac{MPC}{MPS}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{0.9}{0.1}\\\\
&=-9\end{align}$

choices.content: Increase the money supply by $\$800$ billion

assessment_items.question_content:
An economy is currently producing real GDP that is $\$500$ billion more than its full employment output. It is also experiencing an undesirable amount of inflation. Its marginal propensity to consume is $0.75$. 

**Assuming that this is a closed economy with no crowding out, which of the following best describes the impact that a $\$100$ billion increase in government spending will have on this economy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Aggregate demand and real output will eventually increase by only $\$400$ billion, and the price level will increase
Aggregate demand and real output will eventually increase by only $\$300$ billion, and the price level will increase
Aggregate demand and real output will eventually increase by only $\$300$ billion, and the price level will decrease
Aggregate supply and real output will eventually increase by only $\$200$ billion, and the price level will decrease
Aggregate supply and real output will eventually increase by only $\$400$ billion, and the price level will decrease

assessment_items.question_content:
The nation of Xela was in long-run equilibrium but experienced the aggregate demand shock shown below. The marginal propensity to consume (MPC) inXela is $0.9$.

[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following is a fiscal policy action that would be the best choice to return the economy to full employment output?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Xela has a positive output gap of $\$20$ million. In order to close this gap, real GDP must fall by $\$20$ million.

A marginal propensity to consume of $0.9$ means that the government spending multiplier will be $10$:

$\begin{align} \text{government spending multiplier}&=\dfrac{1}{1-MPC}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{1-0.9}
\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{0.10}\\
\\
&=10
\end{align}$

The tax multiplier is always one less than the government spending multiplier, and negative:

$\begin{align} \text{government spending multiplier}&=-\dfrac{MPC}{MPS}\\
\\
&=-\dfrac{0.9}{0.1}
\\
\\
&=-9
\end{align}$

To find out how much of a change is necessary, divide the amount of the change needed to close a gap by the appropriate multiplier:

$\begin{align}\text{Amount of government spending needed}&=\dfrac{\text{size of gap}}{\text{government spending multiplier}}\\
\\
\\
\text{Amount of tax change needed}&=\dfrac{\text{gap to close}}{\text{tax multiplier}}
\end{align}$

options.alt: A coordinate plane plots four labeled straight lines: A D subscript 1, A D subscript 2, S R A S, and L R A S. The horizontal axis is labeled r G D P, and the vertical axis is labeled P L. The horizontal axis is marked at 100 million and 120 million, and the vertical axis is marked at 105 and 110. Line L R A S is vertical and intersects the horizontal axis at 120 million. Line A D subscript 1 is downward-sloping from top left to bottom right. Line A D subscript 2 is also downward-sloping and lies to the left of A D subscript 1. Line S R A S is upward-sloping from bottom left to top right and intersects both A D curves. A rightward arrow is placed between the intersections. A D subscript 2 intersects S R A S at price level 105 and output level 100 million. A D subscript 1 intersects S R A S at price level 110 and output level 120 million. Horizontal dashed lines extend from the price levels to the vertical axis. Vertical dashed lines drop from the output levels to the horizontal axis.

assessment_items.question_content:
An economy is currently producing real GDP that is $\$500$ billion more than its full employment output. It is also experiencing an undesirable amount of inflation.

**If the economy’s marginal propensity to consume is $0.5$ and there is no crowding out, the government should do which of the following fiscal policy actions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
A marginal propensity to consume of $0.5$ means that the government spending multiplier will be $2$:

$\begin{align} \text{government spending multiplier}&=\dfrac{1}{1-MPC}\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{1-0.5}
\\
\\
&=\dfrac{1}{0.5}\\
\\
&=2
\end{align}$

The tax multiplier is always one less than the government spending multiplier, and negative:

$\begin{align} \text{tax multiplier}&=-\dfrac{MPC}{MPS}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{0.5}{0.5}\\\\
&=-1\end{align}$

choices.content: Decrease the money supply by $\$500$ billion

assessment_items.question_content:
An economy is currently producing real GDP that is $\$500$ billion less than its full employment output. It is also experiencing an undesirable amount of inflation. Its marginal propensity to consume is $0.8$. 

**Assuming that this is a closed economy with no crowding out, which of the following best describes the impact that a $\$100$ billion increase in government spending will have on this economy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Aggregate demand and real output will eventually increase by $\$500$ billion, and the price level will increase
Aggregate demand and real output will eventually increase by only $\$300$ billion, and the price level will increase
Aggregate demand and real output will eventually increase by $\$400$ billion, and the price level will decrease
Aggregate supply and real output will eventually increase by only $\$100$ billion, and the price level will decrease
Aggregate supply and real output will eventually increase by only $\$500$ billion, and the price level will decrease
